items.front: When may altitudes above those shown on a military high altitude instrument approach procedure be specified?
items.back: When they are required for separation.

items.front: Who coordinates the maximum altitudes that may be assigned on the military high altitude approach procedure?
items.back: The ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.

items.front: Why are coordinated maximum assignable altitudes established for the procedure?
items.back: To prevent aircraft from exceeding descent-rate or airspeed limitations when above-charted altitudes are assigned.

items.front: If separation requires an altitude above the charted value on the military high altitude procedure, what still limits the assignment?
items.back: The coordinated maximum assignable altitude for that procedure.

items.front: When must the altitude shown in the approach procedure be specified in the approach clearance?
items.back: When adherence to that altitude is required for separation.

items.front: When may the controller omit specifying the altitude in the approach clearance?
items.back: When vertical separation will be provided by the pilot's adherence to the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.

items.front: Which published-altitude types can support omission of the altitude from the approach clearance?
items.back: Published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.

items.front: If the published altitude is required for separation, may it be omitted because it is already on the chart?
What chart family should be used when applying this altitude-specifying rule?
items.back: FAA or NGA instrument approach procedure charts appropriate for the aircraft executing the approach.

items.front: At what airports may circling approach instructions be given?
items.back: Only at airports with operational control towers.

items.front: If landing will be made on a runway other than the one aligned with the direction of instrument approach, what must be included in the clearance?
items.back: Instructions to circle to the runway in use.

items.front: When the direction of the circling maneuver is required, what must ATC state?
items.back: The direction using one of eight cardinal compass points and a left or right base or downwind leg as appropriate.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to direct a circling maneuver to a different runway?
items.back: Circle to Runway (number), or Circle (direction) of the airport or runway for a left or right base or downwind to Runway (number).

items.front: What kind of circling instruction must not be issued if it could take the aircraft outside the protected area?
items.back: An instruction such as "extend downwind leg."

items.front: If a published circling restriction conflicts with the controller's proposed maneuver, which one governs?
items.back: The published circling restriction governs; ATC should not issue contradictory instructions.
